APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper sits well within the scope of top NLP/ML venues that accept formal-language and parsing advances: it proposes a new constrained grammar class (RGL), proves closure properties, and gives a linear-time top-down Earley-like parser, and even evaluates on NLP graph corpora (AMR, UCCA, PDT). From a methodology-auditor perspective the work targets the right audience, but empirical evaluation standards expected at many ML/NLP conferences are only partially met—no baselines, metrics, or code are reported—which raises reproducibility and comparative-evaluation concerns. Overall the theoretical relevance and the applied use on standard graph-structured NLP datasets make it a good fit, albeit with minor scope concerns about empirical validation. | B(4): This paper sits largely within the NLP/ML remit because it addresses graph-based semantic representations (AMR, UCCA, Prague treebank) and provides a practical parsing algorithm with linear-time guarantees under realistic assumptions, which will interest practitioners and theorists alike. However, its heavy emphasis on formal-language contributions (defining RGGs, MSOL/HRL relationships, and closure properties) makes it somewhat theory-centric; while valuable, that orientation may be better appreciated by specialized formal-language venues. Overall the combination of novel formal results plus concrete parsing implications for semantic graph formalisms makes it a good fit for a top NLP/ML conference, albeit with minor scope concerns about audience balance.

From a methodology-auditor perspective the work targets the right audience, but empirical evaluation standards expected at many ML/NLP conferences are only partially met—no baselines, metrics, or code are reported—which raises reproducibility and comparative-evaluation concerns. Overall the theoretical relevance and the applied use on standard graph-structured NLP datasets make it a good fit, albeit with minor scope concerns about empirical validation.

Overall the combination of novel formal results plus concrete parsing implications for semantic graph formalisms makes it a good fit for a top NLP/ML conference, albeit with minor scope concerns about audience balance.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper states clear theoretical claims (RGL subset relations, intersection closure, and a top-down Earley-like parser with linear-time guarantees) and appears to provide proofs and an algorithm, but the summary indicates important assumptions (constraints C1/C2, fixed edge orders, identifiable external node) that may be strong and could limit generality; I cannot verify proof completeness from the summary. The parsing runtime guarantee hinges on implementation-level constraints that need more explicit justification and reproducible pseudocode/complexity analysis. Empirically, the evaluation is thin: datasets are listed but no baselines, metrics, or statistical analyses are reported, so experimental support for practical advantages is insufficient. | B(3): From the summary, the paper states and (apparently) proves formal properties (RGL ⊆ HRL ∩ MSOL, intersection closure) and presents a top-down Earley-like parser with linear-time claims under explicit assumptions; these are plausible but hinge on nontrivial constraints (C1/C2, rank≥1) and the identifiable external-node marker. The experimental methodology is weak: datasets are listed but no baselines, metrics, or statistical analysis are reported, so empirical support for practical advantages is insufficient. There are potential logical gaps in generality of the linear-time claim (depends on fixed edge orders and marker assumption) and in how restrictive C2 affects applicability; therefore I judge the technical contribution as promising but with notable unresolved concerns about empirical validation and the scope of the theoretical claims.

The parsing runtime guarantee hinges on implementation-level constraints that need more explicit justification and reproducible pseudocode/complexity analysis. Empirically, the evaluation is thin: datasets are listed but no baselines, metrics, or statistical analyses are reported, so experimental support for practical advantages is insufficient.

The experimental methodology is weak: datasets are listed but no baselines, metrics, or statistical analysis are reported, so empirical support for practical advantages is insufficient. There are potential logical gaps in generality of the linear-time claim (depends on fixed edge orders and marker assumption) and in how restrictive C2 affects applicability; therefore I judge the technical contribution as promising but with notable unresolved concerns about empirical validation and the scope of the theoretical claims.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper tackles an important problem—efficient, provably tractable parsing for a class of graph languages—by introducing Regular Graph Grammars (RGLs), proving closure properties (intersection) and relating RGLs to HRG and MSOL, and by giving a top-down Earley-like algorithm with linear-time guarantees under clear assumptions. From my methodology-focused perspective, the combination of theoretical guarantees and parsing efficiency materially advances how researchers can approach graph parsing and motivates follow-up work, though practical uptake may be tempered by the C2 constraints that can make grammars unnatural and the parser’s reliance on an external node marker. Overall this is a strong contribution that is likely to influence research on structured graph parsing and inspire extensions to relax assumptions or demonstrate empirical applicability. | B(4): The paper tackles an important practical-theoretical gap by identifying Regular Graph Languages (RGLs) as a constrained subfamily of HRG/MSOL that preserves useful closure properties (notably intersection) while enabling an efficient top-down Earley-like parser with linear-time guarantees under reasonable assumptions. This combination — formal characterization, closure results, and a concrete efficient parsing algorithm tested on AMR/UCCA/dependency data — is likely to change how researchers approach graph parsing by offering a tractable alternative to general HRG. The impact is tempered by the noted expressivity constraints (C2) and the parser’s marker assumption, which may limit adoption in some applications, but overall the work opens significant, practical research directions bridging theory and implementable parsers.

From my methodology-focused perspective, the combination of theoretical guarantees and parsing efficiency materially advances how researchers can approach graph parsing and motivates follow-up work, though practical uptake may be tempered by the C2 constraints that can make grammars unnatural and the parser’s reliance on an external node marker. Overall this is a strong contribution that is likely to influence research on structured graph parsing and inspire extensions to relax assumptions or demonstrate empirical applicability.

This combination — formal characterization, closure results, and a concrete efficient parsing algorithm tested on AMR/UCCA/dependency data — is likely to change how researchers approach graph parsing by offering a tractable alternative to general HRG. The impact is tempered by the noted expressivity constraints (C2) and the parser’s marker assumption, which may limit adoption in some applications, but overall the work opens significant, practical research directions bridging theory and implementable parsers.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (definition of RGG, closure results, and an Earley-like linear-time parser) are stated early and carried through the methods section. Notation and definitions appear consistent and the writing quality is good; related work coverage is thorough which helps situate the claims. However, the presentation of the parser relies on several technical assumptions (fixed edge orders, identifiable marker) that could use more explicit, step-by-step exposition, and the experimental/empirical sections lack detail on evaluation metrics and baselines which makes that part harder to assess from the text alone. | B(4): The manuscript is generally well organized and reads clearly: the methodology (definition of RGG with constraints, relations to HRL/MSOL, and the Earley-like parser) is presented in a logical progression and the main contributions and limitations are stated early. Notation appears consistent and the paper provides thorough related-work grounding; practical assumptions (marker requirement) and constraints (C2) are explicitly discussed, which aids comprehension. While figures/examples and dataset mentions (AMR, UCCA, Prague treebank) support the exposition, the absence of empirical baselines and detailed evaluation metrics slightly reduces clarity about experimental intent. Overall the writing is good with only minor presentation issues that prevent it from being exemplary.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (definition of RGG, closure results, and an Earley-like linear-time parser) are stated early and carried through the methods section. Notation and definitions appear consistent and the writing quality is good; related work coverage is thorough which helps situate the claims. However, the presentation of the parser relies on several technical assumptions (fixed edge orders, identifiable marker) that could use more explicit, step-by-step exposition, and the experimental/empirical sections lack detail on evaluation metrics and baselines which makes that part harder to assess from the text alone.

While figures/examples and dataset mentions (AMR, UCCA, Prague treebank) support the exposition, the absence of empirical baselines and detailed evaluation metrics slightly reduces clarity about experimental intent. Overall the writing is good with only minor presentation issues that prevent it from being exemplary.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): The paper does not include empirical comparisons or baselines (summary states "Baselines compared: none mentioned") despite close prior work on HRG-based graph parsing and AMR (e.g., Parsing Graphs with Hyperedge Replacement Grammars [2], HRG approaches to AMR [1]). As a result, there are no shared datasets/metrics or fairness checks reported, and no acknowledgement of when prior HRG methods might match or outperform the proposed RGG parser. The manuscript does, however, appear to situate itself in the literature (related work coverage = thorough), but this theoretical grounding is not supplemented by comparative experiments or ablation analyses against the most relevant competing approaches. | B(2): Although the manuscript situates itself well within the HRG/MSOL literature and cites relevant prior work (e.g. HRG-based AMR and parsing approaches), it includes no empirical baselines or quantitative comparisons against those established methods (references like the HRG parsing and AMR-HRG papers are not used as runtime or accuracy baselines). The paper therefore fails to demonstrate that its claimed parsing efficiency and practical advantages are realized in practice under comparable conditions; this omission weakens claims of practical impact despite good theoretical positioning.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper does not include empirical comparisons or baselines (summary states "Baselines compared: none mentioned") despite close prior work on HRG-based graph parsing and AMR (e.g., Parsing Graphs with Hyperedge Replacement Grammars [2], HRG approaches to AMR [1]). As a result, there are no shared datasets/metrics or fairness checks reported, and no acknowledgement of when prior HRG methods might match or outperform the proposed RGG parser. The manuscript does, however, appear to situate itself in the literature (related work coverage = thorough), but this theoretical grounding is not supplemented by comparative experiments or ablation analyses against the most relevant competing approaches.

The paper therefore fails to demonstrate that its claimed parsing efficiency and practical advantages are realized in practice under comparable conditions; this omission weakens claims of practical impact despite good theoretical positioning.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable and well-differentiated contribution: it isolates Regular Graph Grammars (RGG) as a constrained subclass of HRG/MSOL and proves properties (intersection-closure) while giving a novel top-down Earley-like linear-time parser under explicit assumptions. These ideas extend prior HRG parsing work (e.g., Chiang et al. 2013 for HRG/CKY-style and Drewes et al. 2015 on predictive top-down subclasses) in a non-trivial way by leveraging rank≥1/external-node constraints to obtain better parsing complexity. An expert familiar with HRG-based AMR and HRG parsing (e.g., 2013/2015 lines of work and Courcelle’s foundational results) would find the focused RGG formalization and resulting algorithm an informative and original refinement rather than a mere recombination of known techniques. | B(4): The paper contributes a notable and coherent new perspective by isolating Regular Graph Grammars (RGG) as a constrained subclass of HRG with provable MSOL/HRL relationships and an intersection-closure result—claims that are not evidently present in the cited HRG parsing literature (e.g. Chiang et al. 2013 / Parsing Graphs with HRG) nor in applied HRG work on AMR. The design of a top-down Earley-style parser that leverages the rank≥1/external-node constraint and yields linear-time behavior under realistic assumptions is a non-trivial methodological advance beyond prior HRG parsing approaches, while still building appropriately on Courcelle’s and Drewes et al.’s lineage. Given these elements, the contribution is clearly differentiated and would be informative to experts, though it is not a radical departure from existing formalisms and therefore stops short of maximal novelty.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable and well-differentiated contribution: it isolates Regular Graph Grammars (RGG) as a constrained subclass of HRG/MSOL and proves properties (intersection-closure) while giving a novel top-down Earley-like linear-time parser under explicit assumptions. These ideas extend prior HRG parsing work (e.g., Chiang et al. 2013 for HRG/CKY-style and Drewes et al. 2015 on predictive top-down subclasses) in a non-trivial way by leveraging rank≥1/external-node constraints to obtain better parsing complexity. An expert familiar with HRG-based AMR and HRG parsing (e.g., 2013/2015 lines of work and Courcelle’s foundational results) would find the focused RGG formalization and resulting algorithm an informative and original refinement rather than a mere recombination of known techniques.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper contributes a notable and coherent new perspective by isolating Regular Graph Grammars (RGG) as a constrained subclass of HRG with provable MSOL/HRL relationships and an intersection-closure result—claims that are not evidently present in the cited HRG parsing literature (e.g. Chiang et al. 2013 / Parsing Graphs with HRG) nor in applied HRG work on AMR. The design of a top-down Earley-style parser that leverages the rank≥1/external-node constraint and yields linear-time behavior under realistic assumptions is a non-trivial methodological advance beyond prior HRG parsing approaches, while still building appropriately on Courcelle’s and Drewes et al.’s lineage. Given these elements, the contribution is clearly differentiated and would be informative to experts, though it is not a radical departure from existing formalisms and therefore stops short of maximal novelty.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): The paper makes substantial formal contributions and reportedly provides an Earley-like linear-time parsing algorithm, but the empirical evaluation is shallow: no baselines, unspecified metrics, and no ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported despite nontrivial algorithmic assumptions (marker availability, fixed edge order, constraints C1/C2). Related work coverage is described as thorough and some limitations are acknowledged, but reproducibility is weak (no code, hyperparameters, or clear experimental protocol reported) and key practical claims about parsing efficiency lack rigorous experimental support against prior HRG-based methods (e.g., [1],[2]). Overall the methodological and empirical validation is underdeveloped relative to the paper’s algorithmic claims. | B(2): The empirical component is shallow: although three graph corpora (AMR, Prague, UCCA) are used, no baselines, evaluation metrics, or quantitative comparisons to existing HRG-based parsers (e.g. works on HRG parsing and AMR with HRG [1,2]) are reported, so claims of practical advantage are under-supported. No ablation or sensitivity analyses are described, and key reproducibility artifacts (code, hyperparameters) are not provided. The related-work coverage is reported as thorough, and the paper does discuss limitations (notably constraint C2 and the external-node marker assumption), but overall experimental and reproducibility evidence is insufficient to substantiate the practical claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper makes substantial formal contributions and reportedly provides an Earley-like linear-time parsing algorithm, but the empirical evaluation is shallow: no baselines, unspecified metrics, and no ablation or sensitivity analyses are reported despite nontrivial algorithmic assumptions (marker availability, fixed edge order, constraints C1/C2). Related work coverage is described as thorough and some limitations are acknowledged, but reproducibility is weak (no code, hyperparameters, or clear experimental protocol reported) and key practical claims about parsing efficiency lack rigorous experimental support against prior HRG-based methods (e.g., [1],[2]). Overall the methodological and empirical validation is underdeveloped relative to the paper’s algorithmic claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The empirical component is shallow: although three graph corpora (AMR, Prague, UCCA) are used, no baselines, evaluation metrics, or quantitative comparisons to existing HRG-based parsers (e.g. works on HRG parsing and AMR with HRG [1,2]) are reported, so claims of practical advantage are under-supported. No ablation or sensitivity analyses are described, and key reproducibility artifacts (code, hyperparameters) are not provided. The related-work coverage is reported as thorough, and the paper does discuss limitations (notably constraint C2 and the external-node marker assumption), but overall experimental and reproducibility evidence is insufficient to substantiate the practical claims.

main_claims: Regular graph languages (RGLs), defined via regular graph grammars (RGG), are a subfamily of both hyperedge replacement languages (HRL) and monadic second-order languages (MSOL) and thus inherit probabilistic and intersection-closure properties.
RGLs are closed under intersection (the paper proves closure under intersection).
The paper presents an efficient top-down parsing algorithm for RGGs (generalizing Earley’s algorithm) with runtime linear in the size of the input graph under stated assumptions.
The paper shows RGGs admit practical parsing advantages over general HRG due to their constraints, enabling more efficient parsing while retaining useful expressive power.
_summary.methodology: Define Regular Graph Grammars as a restricted form of Hyperedge Replacement Grammars (imposing constraints C1 and C2 and rank≥1), relate RGLs to MSOL and HRL, and design a top-down Earley-like parsing algorithm using boundary representations and fixed edge orders to obtain a linear-time parser.
limitations: RGG constraints (particularly C2) can make grammars unnatural and may limit expressivity (may be too constrained for some natural derivations).
The parsing algorithm assumes an identifiable external node (marker) in the input graph, which is a mild assumption but required for the presented parser.
_summary.topic_area: NLP / Formal languages – graph grammars and parsing (Regular Graph Grammars, HRG, MSOL)

reviewer_a.name: Methodology Auditor
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML systems engineer with 8+ years building and evaluating parsing and structured prediction systems; experienced in reproducible research, benchmarking, and algorithm implementation for graph-based models.
traits: Checks for clear algorithmic assumptions, complexity analyses, and whether stated runtime guarantees match the implementation/setting
Looks for reproducibility: pseudocode, implementation details, datasets, and access to code or buildable artifacts
Values empirical validation of claims through baselines, ablations, and sensitivity analyses where applicable
Examines clarity of definitions and whether proofs/sketches omit critical implementation-level constraints (e.g., ordering, boundary handling)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Practical, detail-oriented reviewer who verifies that theoretical claims are supported by precise assumptions and that the work can be reproduced and fairly compared in practice.

reviewer_b.name: Formal-Systems & Impact Specialist
reviewer_b.background: Professor of formal languages and NLP with extensive publications on graph grammars, MSO logic, and parsing theory; focuses on novelty, connections to prior work, and potential impact on research directions and applications.
traits: Evaluates the conceptual novelty relative to HRG/HRL, MSOL characterizations, and related grammar formalisms
Assesses the significance of closure results and how restrictive constraints affect expressive power in practice
Values clear positioning within the literature and articulation of which applications gain from the proposed trade-offs
Pays attention to proof soundness, formal reductions, and whether the proposed class genuinely bridges theory and practical parsing
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Context-aware, literature-driven reviewer who judges contributions by their novelty, theoretical soundness, and potential to influence future work in graph grammars and parsing.
